canonical_examples.analysis: Behind the veil of ignorance, a rational planner does not know whether they will be a wealthy homeowner, a low-income renter, or a new immigrant. From behind the veil, they cannot rationally risk being the low-income resident locked out of affordable housing near jobs. Principles that protect access to affordable housing and mobility — even at some cost to property values — are what rational choosers would select, because those principles protect whoever turns out to be in the worst position.
canonical_examples.problem: A city is designing its zoning policy. Wealthy neighborhoods want strict zoning that limits density; poorer residents want affordable housing near employment centers.

canonical_examples.analysis: The veil of ignorance asks: if you did not know whether you would be the primary caregiver, the non-caregiving employee, or the employer bearing the cost, what policy would you choose? Not knowing your sex, family structure, or career stage, a rational person would choose more generous and flexible leave — because being the primary caregiver with minimal leave is a severe disadvantage, and behind the veil you cannot rule out occupying that position.
canonical_examples.problem: A company is setting its parental leave policy. Mostly male leadership proposes minimal leave.

core_claims: Fair principles of justice must be chosen from behind a 'veil of ignorance' that strips away knowledge of one's own social position, talents, values, and life plan.
The original position — the hypothetical choice situation behind the veil — models impartiality by ensuring that self-interest cannot bias the selection of principles.

Rational agents in the original position would choose Rawls's two principles of justice: equal basic liberties, and that inequalities are only justified if they benefit the least-advantaged members of society (the difference principle).
The veil ensures that choosing principles is equivalent to choosing them fairly, because no one can tailor principles to favor their own known characteristics.
This is a device of representation, not a claim that actual deliberators can or should blank out their identities — it models what fair principles would look like.
The resulting principles reflect a reasonable risk-aversion (maximin): behind the veil, rational choosers protect against the worst possible position they might occupy.
description: The Veil of Ignorance is a thought experiment devised by John Rawls in which principles of justice are chosen by rational agents who do not know their own place in society — their class, sex, race, natural talents, or conception of the good. By eliminating self-interested bias, the device generates principles that are fair to all because they are chosen by none who knows their own advantage.
failure_modes: Stripping away identity may strip away morally relevant particulars: critics (notably communitarians like Sandel) argue that self-knowledge is constitutive of the self, so a 'veil' that removes it produces an artificially thin and unrealistic model of persons.

The maximin rule may be too risk-averse: selecting the option that maximizes the minimum is not the only rational strategy behind the veil; a more risk-neutral agent might choose utilitarian principles instead.

Applicable only to institutional principles, not personal decisions: the veil is a device for choosing the basic structure of society, not for adjudicating individual ethical choices or personal relationships.

key_vocabulary.Difference principle: The second part of Rawls's second principle of justice: social and economic inequalities are just only if they are to the greatest benefit of the least-advantaged members of society.
key_vocabulary.Justice as fairness: Rawls's name for his theory: principles of justice are those that would be agreed upon in conditions of fairness (the original position).
key_vocabulary.Maximin: A decision rule that selects the option whose worst possible outcome is best; Rawls argues parties behind the veil would choose principles using maximin due to the stakes and their ignorance of their position.
key_vocabulary.Original position: Rawls's hypothetical choice situation in which rational agents select principles of justice under the veil of ignorance, without knowledge of their own social position or conception of the good.
key_vocabulary.Primary goods: The goods all rational persons are assumed to want regardless of their specific life plan (rights, liberties, opportunities, income, wealth, the social bases of self-respect) — the currency of Rawlsian justice.
key_vocabulary.Veil of ignorance: The stipulated ignorance in the original position: parties do not know their class, race, sex, natural abilities, psychological predispositions, or conception of the good.

name: Veil of Ignorance
operative_procedure: Establish the choice situation. Frame the problem as one about principles: what rule or policy should govern this type of situation? Translate the specific decision into a question about fair general principles that will apply to everyone in the relevant group.

Apply the veil. Instruct the user to reason as if they did not know which position in the situation they would occupy — whether they would be the employer or the employee, the majority or the minority, the talented or the disadvantaged, the beneficiary or the burdened.
Identify the range of positions. List the distinct social positions that the principle will affect and the range of advantages and disadvantages each position entails under the candidate principles.

Reason from maximin. Ask: which principle would a rational person choose if they might end up in the worst-off position? Rawls's insight is that behind the veil, fairness requires protecting those who would be least advantaged — a rational chooser cannot afford to gamble with the bottom.

Test for justifiability to all. Would the principle be acceptable to someone in the least-advantaged position, the most-advantaged position, and all positions in between? A principle that no one behind the veil would reject has prima facie claim to fairness.
Synthesize. State the principle(s) that emerge and explain why they are fair — because they were chosen under conditions that eliminate bias — then apply them to the user's original problem.
scope_and_limits: The Veil of Ignorance is a powerful tool for testing fairness in institutional design, policy, and rule-making — wherever the question is 'what principles should govern a practice that affects people with different interests?' It is particularly useful for revealing when proposed rules are self-servingly biased. It is not well-suited to personal ethics, relationships, or decisions that turn on particular facts about the parties' identities and histories. It presupposes liberal individualist assumptions that communitarians and some care ethicists dispute.
source_summary: The veil of ignorance was introduced by John Rawls in 'A Theory of Justice' (1971) as the centerpiece of his 'original position' — a hypothetical choice situation designed to model fair conditions for selecting principles of justice. Rawls argued that rational parties behind the veil, using maximin reasoning, would choose two principles: equal basic liberties and the difference principle. He developed and refined the account in 'Political Liberalism' (1993) and 'Justice as Fairness: A Restatement' (2001). Critics include Robert Nozick (from a libertarian direction in 'Anarchy, State, and Utopia'), Michael Sandel (from a communitarian direction in 'Liberalism and the Limits of Justice'), and G.A. Cohen (arguing the difference principle does not go far enough in addressing inequality).

source_texts.title: A Theory of Justice (1971) — John Rawls
